title: Chapter 12
text: Chapter Nine L ara woke to rumbling in her stomach. Scowling, she pressed a hand to her belly. Just a little longer, then she’d get up and start her day. The scrap wasn’t going anywhere. Just a few more minutes away from the real world … As she rolled over to go back to sleep, she realized that her pallet was too soft, and had a strange, sweet smell . She opened her eyes, squinting as their blurriness cleared. Sunlight filtered in through the window coverings, falling in thin lines over the shaggy floor, brightening the white walls. She sat up with a start. Her heartbeat slowed as she remembered where she was; there was her bag on the floor, and there the box of drawers with her treasures arranged atop it. The door was closed, the lock set . Swinging her legs over the side of the bed, she stood and walked to the window, delighting in how the fuzzy flooring tickled her toes. Moving the curtain aside, she pushed down some of the plastic slats and peered out. The trees across the street — she’d never known they could be so tall or have so many leaves — swayed in the breeze. Beyond them, green grass stretched on and on, into eternity . Puddles on the road reflected the hazy sky. Most of the moisture must have been dried up in the sun, which was already high and bright . How late had she slept ? Her stomach twisted on itself, reminding her it was empty. After all she’d eaten the night before, how could she be so hungry ? She went to the dresser and finished off all the leftover food except a strip of the dried meat. That, she wrapped up tight and put in the bottom drawer. Not the best hiding place, but it was better than nothing. Her next meal was never guaranteed . Lara pressed her ear to the door and listened. There were no sounds beyond. Was he up? Was he even home ? “Don’t be such a coward,” she muttered . They had a bargain, and she couldn’t hide from him all day. Sooner or later, he’d come knocking. Who was she to say he wouldn’t break down the door if she pulled that again ? Turning the little switch on the knob, she cracked the door and peered into the hall. Ronin’s bedroom was open, but he was nowhere to be seen. She hurried to the toilet room and locked the door behind her . After relieving herself, Lara studied her reflection in the mirror, frowning at her hair. It was a mess. She combed her fingers through it, wincing as she tugged at the tangles. When she was done, she worked it into a sloppy braid; at least it wasn’t sticking straight up in the air . Her clothing was still damp, so she left it hanging on the curtain rod. Ronin’s shirt would have to do for a while longer . Lara slipped into the hallway and crept downstairs . Ronin sat at the table in the main room, tools spread out before him. He held her boot in one hand . She furrowed her brow. Where had she left her boots last night? She always kept them close to her pallet; they were probably her most valuable possession, usually the first thing she put on in the morning. But today, she hadn’t even looked for them. Was she already going soft ? “What are you doing?” she asked as she reached the bottom step . “Maintaining your gear.” He didn’t look up from the needle he was pulling through the sole of her boot . “Maintaining my… Oh. Um, thanks .” “Care for them properly, and things like this won’t happen .” “It’s not like people go around teaching us how to fix boots and all that .” “At some point,” the needle came back around, drawn through the thick sole as easily as if it were paper, “there was a pair of these that were the first. Nobody told their owner how to take care of them. That person had to learn on their own .” “I don’t need you already busting my—” Lara snapped her mouth shut and took a deep breath, closing her eyes. “Look, I don’t want to fight again , okay ?” When she opened her eyes, he was looking at her. She couldn’t quite read his expression, but there seemed to be a little I wasn’t the one fighting in it, and that almost pissed her off again . “Sleep all right?” he asked, turning back the boot. His hands continued their sure movements, each stitch identical to the last . “I slept late. Didn’t you say you were leaving today ?” “You didn’t seem too keen on my going. Figured you’d be more comfortable if you weren’t alone when you woke up. I know it can take some time to get acclimated to a new environment .” “That’s…thoughtful of you.” She was still reeling from his apology the night before, and now he was taking her feelings into account? “Does it take you time to get used to new places ?” Ronin shifted her boot to a different angle. Lara was amazed at how nimble and precise his metal fingers were . “Yes. Every place has its own leaders. Its own rules. Scrap that’s valuable in one town is worthless in another. And it’s constantly changing. It always takes time to learn all that again .” Reluctantly, she stepped from the soft flooring of the stairs and onto the cold maybe-wood of the main room. “I’m not even strong enough to do what you’re doing now .” “It’s not about how much strength you have, but knowing how much to use,” he replied. He tied off and cut the thread, placed the boot on the table, and turned to face her . “What?” Maybe she should cut him some slack for not understanding her emotions — she couldn’t understand what he was talking about half the time . “The application of strength is more important than the amount of it. It’s just as easy to use too much as it is to use too little, and that often leads to unintended consequences.” He dipped a hand into his pocket, removing a small piece of plastic with little metal dots and lines on it. “Pinch this between your fingers .” She arched a skeptical brow at him as she took it, edges between pointer finger and thumb, and pinched. It was harder than it looked at first glance . “Still not getting it .” “Squeeze. Hard as you can .” Lara did as he said. The plastic didn’t buckle at all, instead digging painfully into her fingertips. When he took it back from her, she was all too happy to be rid of it; her fingers throbbed and there were deep indents in her skin . “Don’t see what that proves,” she said, rubbing the pain away . Ronin grasped the plastic the same way she had, holding it up where she could see. Without any apparent effort, his finger and thumb came together, the plastic snapping in half . “Yeah, you’re stronger than me. Already knew that, didn’t we ?” “That’s not the point, Lara. You must exert a conscious effort to apply all your strength. I must do the same to apply only a small portion of it. That requires a significant portion of my sensory input. It can be just as difficult for me not to break something as it is for you to break it .” The warmth drained from Lara’s face as she stared at the broken pieces of plastic between Ronin’s fingers. She understood the lesson, and it called up dark memories of pain, produced phantom aches in her limbs. Her morning meal churned in her gut . She sprinted through the opening into the other room, stumbled around the central counter, and bent over the wash basin. Her stomach heaved, emptying itself. She stood, trembling and clutching the countertop, as the pain faded and the nausea slowly passed . That fucking thing ! It had known what it was doing to her. The strength of its grip had been deliberate, every bruise intentional, the damage to her shoulder just a taste of the bastard’s full power. She’d been in pain for weeks because that’s what it had wanted . Ronin’s boots thumped across the floor. He stopped a few feet behind her . “Don’t touch me,” she snapped without lifting her head. She spat sour-tasting saliva into the basin . “Are you ill ?” “I’m fine.” Lip curling at the stench of her own vomit, she turned on the water and rinsed out the basin. Once it was clear, she cupped her hands beneath the flow and rinsed her mouth . “Appearances suggest otherwise.” Ronin moved to place a hand on the counter to her right, keeping a few feet between them. “Your face is paler than usual .” “I said I’m fine .” She wiped her lips with the back of her hand and straightened. A cramp seized her stomach, but she clenched her jaw and rode it out . So much for the food I ate . “Thanks for fixing my boot .” He maintained his silent stare long enough for her skin to itch beneath it. She shifted her weight from one foot to the other and back again, waiting for him to say something, to say anything . “I said I was fine, dammit!” She marched back into the main room and snatched up her boots . “You told me bots always say what they mean. Is it the opposite for humans ?” He stood in the entryway between the two rooms, still watching her. She should’ve heard judgment in his words, but there was only curiosity. Couldn’t he at least make it easy to be pissed off at him ? “Don’t you have somewhere to go?” she asked. Being here alone wasn’t appealing, but she couldn’t take any more reminders of what had happened right now. Not while she knew those things were all around. Ronin would just keep probing, and it would get her worked up, and damn if she didn’t want to believe his concern. It seemed genuine . But from a bot, it couldn’t be . Ronin frowned, brow creasing. “I can wait if you’re unwell .” “If I have to tell you I’m fine one more time, I’m gonna scream .” He didn’t respond for a long while. Maybe screaming until her throat was raw would make her feel better … “I’ll get back as quickly as I can,” he finally said. “Is there anything, in particular, you need? Apart from the necessities .” “If you need something, it is a necessity,” she replied. The swelling of pride at her own wit dulled the edge on her mood a little . “You are correct.” One corner of his mouth lifted. He took his coat off the back of the chair and pulled it on, metal fingers displaying their dexterity as he buttoned it. “Lock the door behind me .” He picked up his backpack and slung it over his shoulder as he moved to the front entrance . “Wait!” Lara called, just as his hand settled on the knob . Ronin looked over his shoulder . “My sister. Tabitha. You said you’d look for her .” He nodded . “You don’t even know what she looks like .” Again, that silent stare. Lara’s mouth went dry; was it because of the way he looked at her, or because she missed her sister ? “She’s about this tall,” Lara said, holding her hand a few inches over her head, “with short black hair and brown eyes, and darker skin. And, uh…a little…curvier than me.” Especially if all was well and Tabitha was being fed by a bot. “Has a nasty scar on the back of her left hand. She dances…used to dance. At Kitty’s .” If there was anything familiar to him about her description, he made no sign of it. “When did you last see her ?” “A couple months ago.” Lara dropped her gaze to the floor, trying to pull up Tabitha’s face from her memory. Why was it so hard? “She snuck a visit to tell me she was okay, that she was being kept by a bot .” “Isn’t it likely she’s still with that bot , then ?” “Yes…but…I don’t know where, and I haven’t heard from Tabitha since. I’m worried for her.” She met his eyes. Was he capable of sympathy? He’d probably only help because it was a condition of their deal. “I just want to know she’s all right .” “I’ll ask around, if I can. Don’t expect anything right away. The gearheads and their leader don’t seem to care much for curiosity around town .” Lara pressed her lips together and nodded. He’d agreed to try. That was all she could ask, and it would have to be enough . “Lock the door, Lara,” he said softly, and then he was gone . She stared at the closed door for a time, and then, finally, stepped forward and locked it. Pressing her forehead against its cool face, she smirked. Gearheads, he’d called them . For some reason, it reminded her of the night before, when he’d spoken to the gearheads at the gate — there is an agreement in place . She knew those words had been meant for her . Perhaps Ronin was the one bot she might, in time, be able to trust . * * * R onin knew the building had a different name, long ago, but the bots of Cheyenne called it the Clinic. Outwardly, it was a relic of a lost time, sitting within a bulge in the town’s northern wall and separated from the rest of the district by a broad street. Its brick face and tiled roofs would have been old even at the time of the Black Out, and Ronin doubted the bell tower had sounded even once in the last two centuries . It was well-maintained, like everything else in the Bot District, though off-color bricks had been utilized for some of the exterior repairs . The gearheads stood outside the main entrance, conversing in low tones. They didn’t so much as look at Ronin when he walked past them. Though Warlord dipped his hand into everything in Cheyenne, the Clinic operated freely, offering repairs to all bots . The front doors slid open silently at Ronin’s approach. The building’s interior was in stark contrast to the outside; the reception room was all polished floors, sleek furniture, and chrome finish, bathed in the sanitary white glow of the overhead lights . The synth at the front desk — Mercy — greeted him. Her face was framed by short blond hair, and her pink lips seemed locked in a perpetual smile . “Back so soon?” she asked. “I hope you haven’t suffered more damage already .” Regardless of the time of day, Mercy was always at the desk when Ronin came. Did she ever leave? Ever pursue her own entertainment? She was attractive and pleasant, a perfect choice for companionship; there’d be no volatile emotions, no unpredictable outbursts, no fear of accidental injury . So why was she so unappealing to him? Why was Lara the only one to catch and hold his interest in one hundred and eighty-five years ? “Just need some reskinning.” He raised his hands to display their bare casings . The situation with Lara was a business arrangement. There was no expectation of companionship or any deeper relationship. She’d dance, and Ronin would watch. That was it. Why did his analysis continually suggest there was something more ? “The epidermal synthesizer is free, currently,” Mercy said, frowning at Ronin’s hands. Was it an expression of sympathy, or of pity? “You remember the way. Go on back .” He dropped his hands into his pockets and walked down the long, sterile corridor. Of course he remembered; only the truly damaged forgot things after the Black Out . Was that truly a bad thing, though? The Creators had granted bots near-perfect recall. Cursed them to remember every face, every failure, every tragedy … Doors drifted by on either side. The rooms beyond were dark, full of dormant, unfamiliar equipment, and he’d never seen any of them used. The repair stations were deep within the building. Everything else… He guessed that the Clinic had been used primarily for human care, before the Black Out. Flesh was fragile . Apart from routine maintenance, most bots rarely suffered damage enough to require a trip to this place. Dustwalkers were in a class of their own. Whether human or bot, a dustwalker wasn’t likely to make it back into a town without damage of some sort . Warlord’s gearheads — the synths, anyway — preferred to have chunks of skin missing. It spoke a plain message — I am a bot . Ronin turned into the dimly-lit room marked Epidermal Synthesizer . The bot on duty wasn’t unlike the food vendor, Greene, with a similar body shape and white casing. This one had a red plus sign on its chest . “Greetings, Ronin. Are you in need of service ?” “Three patches on my abdomen.” Ronin removed his hands from his pockets and glanced down at them. Lara’s initial hostility had been triggered when she saw their bare metal. Did she return to whatever dark thoughts haunted her every time she saw them? “And my hands .” The attendant worked its small control console. On the far side of the room, the synthesizer hummed to life, its interior chamber lighting up . “Please remove your clothing and enter the Epidermal Synthesizer,” the bot said as the chamber door slid open . Ronin stood his rifle against the wall, settled his pack on the floor beside it, and unfasted the buttons on his coat, draping it over one of the chairs. He added his shirt a moment later, and sat down to untie his boots. He kicked them off and slid them beneath the chair . The quiet clicking of the attendant bot’s fingers on the console was the only sound apart from the whisper of cloth as Ronin removed his pants, folded them over his arm, and added them to the pile . He shifted his optics down. His casing was visible through the holes on his abdomen, gleaming in the light cast by the synthesizer. Random patches of his skin were lighter or darker than the rest, result of dozens of repair jobs in dozens of towns . Though the discolored spots weren’t distinguishable from the rest of his skin in any other way — as smooth to the touch as everywhere else — they reminded him of the scars many organics bore. A subtle, visible history wrought in flesh. He could recall the circumstances behind each damage, no matter how minor, he’d ever suffered . “Please, enter the Epidermal Synthesizer,” the attendant repeated. Its faintly glowing optics were trained on Ronin, and though its face was incapable of expression, it seemed to hesitate. “Have you suffered damage deeper than the surface, Ronin ?” “I’m fine,” Ronin heard himself reply. He forced himself to walk to the chamber. As he entered, he assessed data, pondering the problem awaiting him at his residence, just over a mile away . Damage deeper than the surface . All bots had lost something in the Black Out. Parts of themselves that couldn’t be replaced. It was the sort of damage that couldn’t be repaired at a place like the Clinic, the sort of damage that could wreak subtle havoc on a bot’s core programming . Wasn’t that similar to the mental fragility of humans? Ronin had seen it firsthand in the Dust; humans, especially in stressful situations, often shattered . What hidden damage had Lara suffered? What internal scars did she carry ? The chamber door closed, sealing Ronin inside. It was utterly silent for several seconds. Then the synthesizer’s motors engaged, moving the scanning arms on either side of him. Their lights flickered on — blue, a welcome change from the harsh white that dominated the place, and a reminder of Lara’s eyes — and the arms circled Ronin, bathing him in their glow . Bots and humans were products of the Creators. Their differences were many, but why did synths, when fully repaired and maintained, look so much like humans? Was it simply an aesthetic that appealed to the eye of their makers? Perhaps they weren’t quite as different beneath their appearances as everyone thought . The scanners retracted, replaced by numerous fabrication arms. The synthesizer shifted to full operation, emitting a high whine. The small arms soldered delicate wires into the neural network on his casing, while the large ones, moving slowly, knitted synthetic skin over the top of the wiring . Though perfection was impossible, synths seemed the closest to it of all intelligent beings. Why, then, was a human so interesting to him? Why were her flaws more alluring, her imperfections more natural ? He closed his eyes, reducing his vision to the faint, brown-red glow that filtered through his eyelids. His true programming seemed simultaneously so close and so terribly far away. Pushing aside the various sensory inputs, he delved into his memory core, searching for the missing piece that would unlock what he’d lost . When he stumbled upon the memory of a shack on the edge of the road and the redhaired woman dancing within, he stopped . And played it over and over again .
